project.name: MTM Receiving Application
notes: WinUI 3 desktop app with MVVM, CQRS, and database-backed workflows.
Use this catalog as the single editable source for feature names and suggested code entry points.

commonAccessibilityNeeds: Make the screen easier to scan
Make actions clearer for non-technical users
Keep important actions visible without scrolling
debugFrequencies: Only after a specific workflow path
symptomTypes: Navigation goes to the wrong place
logicConcernTypes: Warning should block but does not
Warning should not block but does

features.name: Bulk Inventory Processing
features.summary: Bulk inventory workflows, automations, and reconciliation logic.

features.name: Settings Core
features.summary: Cross-cutting settings infrastructure: persistence, encryption, user preferences, CQRS commands, and the settings window host.

features.name: Settings — Receiving
features.summary: User-configurable receiving defaults, validation rules, business rules, and per-user preferences for the receiving workflow.

features.name: Settings — Dunnage
features.summary: Dunnage workflow configuration, permissions, audit settings, UI/UX preferences, and user-level options.

features.name: Settings — Reporting
features.summary: Report output paths, email UX, file I/O configuration, business rules, and permissions for the reporting module.

features.name: Settings — Volvo
features.summary: Volvo integration connection strings, file paths, database settings, UI configuration, and externalization backlog.

features.name: Settings — Developer Tools
features.summary: Developer-facing diagnostic settings, database test connectivity, feature toggles (A–D), and settings overview panel.

features.name: ShipRec Tools
features.summary: Shipment-receiving utility tools including outside service history lookup and multi-tool selection workflow.

features.name: Shared Infrastructure
features.summary: Shared ViewModels (base, login, main window, splash screen, help dialog), cross-cutting DI registration, and app-level configuration.

forms.title: UI Change Request
forms.summary: Describe a screen change in plain English, with as much guided picking and as little typing as possible.
whenToUse: Use this when the screen already exists and you want it to look or behave differently.
Use this when layout, grouping, labels, or interactions should change.
Do not use this if the real problem is business logic or a bug with no UI design goal.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can load helpful choices for you.
Think about what feels hard or confusing today.
Know which parts of the workflow must stay the same.
endUserTips: Use plain language first and exact control names second.
Pick from the suggested choices wherever possible so you type less.
Describe the result the user should see, not the code you think should change.

fieldGroups.title: Request Summary
fieldGroups.description: Start with the goal in simple language. The form will help with the technical detail.

fields.label: Short name for this UI change
fields.placeholder: Change Receiving Edit Mode from grid to cards

fields.label: Who is this screen mainly for?
What feels wrong today, and what should be different?
fields.placeholder: Explain the current UI problem in plain English, then say what the user should see instead.

fields.label: What should be easier after this change?
fields.placeholder: Describe what the user should be able to do faster, more clearly, or with less confusion.

fieldGroups.title: Screen And Interaction Detail
fieldGroups.description: Pick the closest known answers first. Only type where you really need to explain something.

fields.label: Which workflow area is this in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Which screen or step should change?
fields.placeholder: Pick the main screen or step

fields.label: What does it mostly look like today?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest current layout

fields.label: What should it mostly look like after the change?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest target layout

fields.label: Which parts of the screen are involved?
What should the user be able to do more easily?
Layout notes in plain English
fields.placeholder: Describe grouping, spacing, emphasis, or what should be more visible.

fields.label: What interaction changes are needed?
fields.placeholder: Describe clicks, taps, navigation, focus order, selection behavior, or validation feedback changes.

fields.label: What text on the screen should change?
fields.placeholder: Add any labels, button text, helper text, or messages that should change.

fields.label: Which usability needs matter here?
Anything else the user may struggle with?
fields.placeholder: Add any extra usability, accessibility, or training concerns here.

fields.label: Suggested files to review first
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely files when a feature is selected.

fields.label: What should stay the same?
Anything else that must stay the same?
fields.placeholder: Add extra constraints only if the suggested choices missed something important.

fields.label: How will we know this UI change is done?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely success checks when a feature is selected.

forms.title: Debugging Request
forms.summary: Describe what is going wrong in simple language, with guided picks wherever possible.
whenToUse: Use this when something is broken, inconsistent, or failing unexpectedly.
Use this when you can describe what happens and what should have happened instead.
Do not use this for a pure redesign or cleanup request.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can load helpful context.
Reproduce the problem at least once if possible.
Capture the exact message or visible symptom if you can.
endUserTips: Facts matter more than guesses.
Use the guided choices first, then add any missing detail in plain English.
Paste exact messages instead of paraphrasing them when possible.

fields.label: Short bug title
fields.placeholder: Receiving load list does not refresh after clear

fields.label: How urgent is this?
Which workflow area is this happening in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Which screen or step shows the problem?
fields.placeholder: Pick the main screen or step

fields.label: Which of these best describe the problem?
What is the user actually seeing?
fields.placeholder: Describe the failure exactly as the user sees it, in plain English.

fields.label: What should have happened instead?
fields.placeholder: Describe the expected result.

fields.label: How can someone make this happen again?
fields.placeholder: One step per line

fields.label: How often does it happen?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest frequency

fields.label: Anything special about when it happens?
fields.placeholder: User role, machine, branch, config, test data, or timing conditions.

fields.label: Exact error message or warning
fields.placeholder: Paste exact messages if available.

fields.label: Logs, traces, or debugger clues
fields.placeholder: Paste log snippets, stack traces, or debugger observations.

fields.label: Do you have a guess about the cause?
fields.placeholder: If you have a theory, describe it. If not, say Unknown.

fields.label: Suggested files or layers to inspect
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely files when a feature is selected.

fields.label: How should we verify the fix worked?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely checks when a feature is selected.

forms.title: Feature Logic Correction
forms.summary: Describe when the feature follows the wrong rules, path, or decision, using guided choices where possible.
whenToUse: Use this when the feature runs but behaves incorrectly according to user intent or business rules.
Use this when the UI may be fine but the workflow decisions are wrong.
Do not use this for styling-only requests.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can load helpful context.
Describe the intended workflow in plain language before describing the bug.
Think about which rule, branch, or condition is wrong.
endUserTips: Focus on intent versus actual behavior.
Use the guided choices first, then explain the mismatch in plain English.
Give examples of good and bad outcomes.
List what must keep working after the correction.

fields.label: Which workflow area is this in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Which screen or step is involved?
fields.placeholder: Pick the main screen or step

fields.label: What kind of logic problem is this?
What is this feature supposed to do?
fields.placeholder: Describe the intended business behavior in plain language.

fields.label: What does it do today instead?
fields.placeholder: Describe the current behavior.

fields.label: Which rule or expectation is being broken?
fields.placeholder: State any rule or assumption the current logic breaks.

fields.label: Which conditions make this matter?
fields.placeholder: One condition per line

fields.label: What data or records affect this behavior?
fields.placeholder: Describe fields, stored procedures, or external data involved.

fields.label: Suggested layers or services involved
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely files or layers from the selected feature.

fields.label: What should stay the same while fixing this?
Any other constraints to preserve?
fields.placeholder: Add extra architecture, performance, or compatibility constraints if needed.

fields.label: What rules should the feature follow instead?
fields.placeholder: One rule per line

fields.label: Which scenarios should prove the fix?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely success checks when a feature is selected.

fields.label: What must not break while fixing this?
fields.placeholder: List related flows that must remain stable.

forms.title: Improvement Refactor Request
forms.summary: Describe a cleanup or refactor request in plain English, with guided choices that help keep the scope realistic.
whenToUse: Use this when the feature mostly works but the code is hard to maintain, follow, or extend.
Use this for cleanup, decomposition, duplication removal, or clearer ownership boundaries.
Do not use this when the main issue is a live bug or wrong business behavior.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can suggest likely files.
Decide how broad the cleanup is allowed to be.
Think about what must stay stable while the structure improves.
endUserTips: Explain why the current code is hard to work with in plain English.
Use the suggested files and constraints where they fit.
Be clear about whether the behavior must stay exactly the same.

fields.label: Which workflow area is this in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Why is this cleanup or refactor needed?
fields.placeholder: Describe what feels too tangled, duplicated, fragile, or hard to change.

fields.label: What are the main pain points today?
fields.placeholder: One pain point per line

fields.label: How broad can this refactor be?
What should the improved code feel like afterward?
fields.placeholder: Describe the desired structure, ownership, or readability improvement.

fields.label: Suggested files or types to review first
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely files from the selected feature.

fields.label: What boundaries or patterns must still be respected?
fields.placeholder: Mention MVVM boundaries, service seams, DAO rules, or dependency concerns.

fields.label: What should stay the same while cleaning this up?
Risk level
What tests or checks should help keep this safe?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely checks from the selected feature.

fields.label: How will we know this cleanup is done?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely success criteria from the selected feature.

forms.title: Logging Refactor Request
forms.summary: Describe how logging should change, using guided picks so you can say more by choosing and less by typing.
whenToUse: Use this when log output is missing, too noisy, inconsistent, or exposing the wrong details.
Use this when the main change is about observability rather than workflow behavior.
Do not use this for general refactors unless logging is the main objective.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can suggest files and events.
Know whether you want more logs, fewer logs, or clearer logs.
Think about what should never appear in logs.
endUserTips: State whether this is for debugging, support, compliance, or noise reduction.
Use the suggested files or events if they match your need.
Be explicit about what should stay, go, or change severity.

fields.label: Which workflow area is this in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: What kind of logging change is this?
Why should the logging change?
fields.placeholder: Describe the debugging, audit, support, or noise problem in plain English.

fields.label: What is wrong with the current logs?
fields.placeholder: Too noisy, too sparse, inconsistent, missing correlation, wrong severity, and so on.

fields.label: What should the logs feel like afterward?
fields.placeholder: Describe the target signal quality, structure, and severity usage.

fields.label: Which events or transitions should be logged?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely events from the selected feature.

fields.label: Which logs should be removed or made quieter?
fields.placeholder: One log pattern per line

fields.label: How should severity be used?
fields.placeholder: Describe what should be Trace, Debug, Information, Warning, or Error.

fields.label: What must never be written to the logs?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill common do-not-log guidance from the selected feature.

fields.label: Suggested files or services to inspect
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely files from the selected feature.

fields.label: How should we check that the logging change worked?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely checks from the selected feature.

forms.title: Code Review Request
forms.summary: Describe what you want reviewed, with guided choices that focus the review on defects, risks, and missing validation.
whenToUse: Use this when you want Copilot to review a feature, module, or set of changes for problems.
Use this when the main goal is finding risks rather than implementing a requested change.
Use this for review of planned or completed work.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can suggest likely files and risks.
State whether this is a broad review or a focused review.
Think about the biggest risks you want reviewed first.
endUserTips: If the codebase area is large, narrow the review to the feature or files that matter most.
Say whether you want critical defects only or a fuller review.
Mention any recent changes or unstable areas the reviewer should keep in mind.

fields.label: Which workflow area is this in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Short review title
fields.placeholder: Review receiving clear-to-history changes

fields.label: How broad should the review be?
Suggested files or areas to review first
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely files from the selected feature.

fields.label: What should the review focus on most?
fields.placeholder: Describe the kinds of problems you most want the review to catch.

fields.label: Which kinds of risks matter most here?
What recent changes or background should the reviewer know?
fields.placeholder: Describe recent edits, PR intent, or unstable areas.

fields.label: What should the review output look like?
fields.placeholder: Example: findings first, highest risk first, file references, and testing gaps.

forms.title: Database Issue Request
forms.summary: Describe a database-related problem in plain English, with guided picks for system, objects, files, and validation.
whenToUse: Use this when the issue is in stored procedures, database mappings, query results, or schema alignment.
Use this when the code problem cannot be understood without database context.
Do not use this for purely visual UI changes.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can suggest likely files and objects.
Identify whether the issue is MySQL or Infor Visual SQL Server.
Capture the exact mismatch, bad result, or SQL error if you can.
endUserTips: Paste exact SQL or stored procedure names when available.
Use the suggested files or database objects if they match.
Mention constraints like read-only Visual access or stored-procedure-only rules.

fields.label: Which workflow area is this in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Short database issue title
fields.placeholder: sp_Receiving_LabelData_Insert missing field mapping

fields.label: Which database system is involved?
Affected procedures, tables, queries, or DAOs
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely database objects from the selected feature.

fields.label: What is going wrong in the database flow?
fields.placeholder: Describe the mismatch, failure, or incorrect data behavior.

fields.label: SQL errors, mismatched data, or examples
fields.placeholder: Paste exact messages, row examples, or result differences.

fields.label: What database rules or limits must be respected?
fields.placeholder: Stored procedures only, read-only Visual DB, no schema changes, and so on.

fields.label: How should we check that the database fix worked?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely checks from the selected feature.

forms.title: Test Generation Request
forms.summary: Describe which code needs tests and what those tests must prove, with guided file and scenario suggestions.
whenToUse: Use this when you want Copilot to add or expand tests for a file, class, or module.
Use this when the main output is tests, not production code changes.
Use this for unit or integration test requests.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can suggest likely files and scenarios.
Decide whether you want unit, integration, or mixed tests.
Think about the few behaviors that matter most.
endUserTips: State the test scope clearly.
Use the suggested files or scenarios if they match your need.
Focus on behaviors that matter more than a coverage number.

fields.label: Which workflow area is this in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Short test request title
fields.placeholder: Add ViewModel tests for receiving workflow

fields.label: What kind of tests do you want?
Suggested files, classes, or modules to test
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely files from the selected feature.

fields.label: What must these tests prove?
fields.placeholder: Describe core behaviors, edge cases, and failure paths.

fields.label: Which dependencies should be mocked or kept real?
fields.placeholder: Describe services, DAOs, database access, or framework dependencies.

fields.label: Which scenarios absolutely need coverage?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely scenarios from the selected feature.

fields.label: What testing rules or limits matter here?
fields.placeholder: Naming patterns, no database access, use FluentAssertions, and so on.

forms.title: Documentation Change Request
forms.summary: Describe a documentation update in plain English, with guided source-file and topic suggestions.
whenToUse: Use this when documentation needs to be created, corrected, or expanded.
Use this when the user goal is clearer guidance for developers, operators, or end users.
Use this when code changes require synchronized docs.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can suggest likely source files and topics.
Know who the finished document is meant to help.
Decide whether the problem is missing docs, stale docs, or confusing docs.
endUserTips: Describe who will read the documentation.
Use the suggested source files or topics if they fit.
If the docs must match code exactly, say so explicitly.

fields.label: Which workflow area is this in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Short documentation request title
fields.placeholder: Update receiving workflow support guide

fields.label: Who is this documentation mainly for?
What is wrong, missing, or unclear today?
fields.placeholder: Describe the missing, stale, or confusing documentation.

fields.label: What should the finished documentation help the reader do?
fields.placeholder: Describe what the finished documentation should help the reader do.

fields.label: Suggested source files or modules to inspect
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely files from the selected feature.

fields.label: Which sections or topics should be included?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely topics from the selected feature.

fields.label: How should we check that the documentation is correct?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely checks from the selected feature.

forms.title: UI Mockup Request
forms.summary: Describe a screen idea or mockup request in plain English, with guided picks for layout, screens, sections, and controls.
whenToUse: Use this when the user wants a new screen concept or a visual mockup before implementation.
Use this when a normal UI change request is not enough because the screen is still exploratory.
Do not use this when the request is only for code cleanup.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can suggest screens, controls, and sections.
Know the screen purpose and main user actions.
Think about any layout or workflow constraints that must stay true.
endUserTips: Describe the screen as if explaining it to a designer.
Use the suggested screens, sections, and controls if they fit.
Mention desktop assumptions if the screen is WinUI-specific.

fields.label: Which workflow area is this for?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Short mockup title
fields.placeholder: Receiving discrepancy entry screen

fields.label: What should this screen help the user do?
fields.placeholder: Describe what this screen helps the user do.

fields.label: What are the most important user actions?
fields.placeholder: One action per line

fields.label: Who is this screen mainly for?
Which known screen or step is this closest to?

fields.label: What does the closest current screen mostly look like?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest current layout

fields.label: What should this mockup mostly look like?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest target layout

fields.label: Which sections or cards should the mockup include?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely sections from the selected feature.

fields.label: Which fields or controls should be shown?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely controls from the selected feature.

fields.label: What layout or visual notes matter most?
fields.placeholder: Describe hierarchy, spacing, panel layout, tables, buttons, and status areas.

fields.label: What constraints should the mockup respect?
fields.placeholder: Window size, desktop assumptions, branding, accessibility, or workflow constraints.
